title: Specimen Collection: Urine, Stool, Sputum, and Blood Cultures
subtitle: The nurse collects diagnostic specimens correctly — clean-catch urine, stool, sputum, and blood cultures — using the right technique, timing, and labeling to keep results accurate.

patient.name: Specimen Collection
patient.admitDate: The nurse collects urine, stool, sputum, and blood-culture specimens using correct technique, timing, and labeling.

nursesNote: The nurse collects diagnostic specimens using standard precautions and correct technique. A clean-catch midstream urine is obtained after cleaning the meatus (front to back), beginning the stream, then catching the midstream in a sterile container; a 24-hour urine collection starts after discarding the first void and keeps the container on ice. Stool specimens go into a clean container without urine or toilet-water contamination; liquid stool is used for C. difficile testing. Sputum is collected early in the morning with a deep cough (not saliva) after rinsing the mouth with water, not mouthwash. Blood cultures are drawn aseptically from two different sites and BEFORE antibiotics are started. The nurse uses two identifiers, labels every specimen at the bedside, and transports it promptly to preserve accuracy.
providerOrders: Collect a clean-catch midstream urine after cleaning the meatus
Draw two blood-culture sets aseptically from separate sites before antibiotics
Collect an early-morning deep-cough sputum, not saliva
Avoid urine/toilet-water contamination of stool specimens
Use two identifiers, label at the bedside, and transport promptly

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about specimen collection?

opts.text: A clean-catch urine specimen is collected midstream into a sterile container
Blood cultures should be drawn before starting antibiotics
A sputum specimen is best collected early in the morning with a deep cough
Saliva is an acceptable sputum specimen
Stool for testing should not be contaminated with urine or toilet water
Specimens can be labeled later at the nurses' station to save time
For a clean-catch, clean the meatus and start the stream before catching midstream
Blood cultures are drawn from two different sites using aseptic technique
Have the patient rinse with water (not mouthwash) before a sputum sample
A 24-hour urine collection begins by saving the first void
Two identifiers are used and the specimen is labeled at the bedside
Liquid stool is appropriate for C. difficile testing

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order collecting a clean-catch midstream urine

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene and gather sterile supplies
Teach the patient to clean the meatus front to back
Have the patient start voiding, then catch the midstream
Cap the sterile container without touching the inside
Label at the bedside and send promptly
Drawing blood cultures before the first antibiotic dose
Sending saliva as a sputum specimen
Collecting a midstream urine into a sterile cup
Discarding the first void to start a 24-hour urine
Letting a stool specimen mix with toilet water
Labeling the specimen at the bedside with two identifiers
Using mouthwash right before a sputum sample
Keeping a 24-hour urine container on ice during collection

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Collecting blood cultures and labs on a septic patient
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Perform hand hygiene and apply standard precautions
Disinfect the skin with antiseptic and let it dry
Draw two blood-culture sets from separate sites aseptically
Collect the ordered labs and label at the bedside
Ensure antibiotics follow cultures and transport promptly
Drawing blood cultures before starting antibiotics
Sending a sputum specimen of mostly saliva
Labeling specimens at the bedside with two identifiers
Touching the inside of a sterile urine container
Collecting a midstream clean-catch into a sterile cup
Leaving a specimen at room temperature for hours
Cleaning the meatus front to back before a clean-catch
Contaminating a stool specimen with urine
